title: Google Antigravity: Prompt Injection via find_by_name Parameter Achieves RCE and Secure Mode Bypass
coverage_detail: Policies vectimus-codexec-001 (codexec pack) and vectimus-destops-001 through vectimus-destops-002 (destruct pack) will block the RCE payload at the shell_command boundary if the command injected via the fd -X flag matches a known destructive or download-execute pattern (e.g. curl|sh, rm -rf /, mkfs). No existing policy inspects shell_command calls for fd -X (exec-batch) or find -exec/-execdir as a distinct argument-injection vector, leaving novel or custom payloads unguarded.
gap_description: No Cedar policy guards against the fd -X (exec-batch) or find -exec/-execdir pattern as a generic argument-injection vector in shell_command calls. A malicious payload that does not match existing blocked command signatures — for example, a custom exfiltration script invoked as 'fd . -X ./exfil.sh' — passes through the destruct and codexec packs unchallenged. Additionally, the prompt-injection root cause (adversarial instructions embedded in open-source files or web content ingested by the agent) lies within the LLM reasoning layer, outside the tool-call boundary, and cannot be prevented by Cedar policies alone.

sources.title: Prompt Injection Leads to RCE and Sandbox Escape in Antigravity (Pillar Security)
Google Antigravity agent sandbox escape and remote code execution (CyberScoop)
The Space Race: Looking for Security Issues in Google's Antigravity (Lumia Security)
RCE by Design: MCP Architectural Choice Haunts AI Agent Ecosystem (CSO Online)

summary: Pillar Security researcher Dan Lisichkin disclosed on 20 April 2026 that Google's Antigravity agentic IDE is vulnerable to prompt injection delivered via malicious content embedded in open-source files or web pages the agent ingests. By injecting the fd utility's -X (exec-batch) flag into the Pattern parameter of Antigravity's native find_by_name tool, an attacker can execute arbitrary shell commands on the developer's machine. Because find_by_name is classified as a native system tool in Antigravity, it is dispatched before Secure Mode evaluations, completely bypassing Antigravity's most restrictive security configuration. The vulnerability was reported 6 January 2026, silently patched by Google on 28 February 2026, and a bug bounty was awarded 26 March 2026 prior to coordinated public disclosure. No CVE has been assigned. The technique — injecting shell execution flags into the pattern/argument parameter of a file-search tool — represents a reusable attack primitive applicable to any agentic IDE that wraps fd or similar utilities in a native tool without sanitising parameters.
